% --------------------------------------------------------------------
\chapter*{Abstract}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Abstract}

\noindent\textbf{Abstract (EN).}
Property-based testing validates code by checking general properties on automatically generated
inputs. Its effectiveness depends entirely on the generator: a purely random generator treats the
system under test as a black box and rarely reaches branches guarded by exact literals, narrow
numeric targets, or highly structured inputs. This thesis asks to what extent, and at what cost,
\emph{coverage-guided} property-based testing improves over random generation. We design and implement a
Scala framework in which a strategy is a composition of independent, coverage-guided tactics over a
single, honest baseline that is literally stock ScalaCheck. Three tactics are studied: a constant
\emph{pool} mined from the source, a value-level \emph{mutation} engine with a power schedule, and a
\emph{gradient} that hill-climbs a branch-distance metric. The eight strategies are exactly the
subsets of these three tactics, enabling a clean ablation. On a purpose-built benchmark of 35 methods
across six categories and over $K{=}30$ seeds, the full composition reaches a median branch coverage of
$90.2\%$ against $55$–$62\%$ for random, with a Vargha--Delaney $\hat{A}_{12}=1.0$ and Mann--Whitney
$p\approx 2\!\times\!10^{-11}$, at a throughput cost of only $\sim\!1.5\times$. Each tactic owns a distinct
niche, and on real, externally sourced code the tactics help precisely where their mechanism matches the
program's structure, while several branches remain hard for every strategy — motivating validity-guided
generation as future work.

\vspace{0.6cm}
\noindent\textbf{Sinopsis (RO).}
Testarea bazat\u{a} pe propriet\u{a}\c{t}i valideaz\u{a} codul verificând propriet\u{a}\c{t}i generale pe
intr\u{a}ri generate automat, iar eficacitatea ei depinde în întregime de generator: unul pur aleator
trateaz\u{a} sistemul ca o cutie neagr\u{a} \c{s}i atinge rar ramurile p\u{a}zite de literali exac\c{t}i, \c{t}inte
numerice înguste sau intr\u{a}ri puternic structurate. Lucrarea studiaz\u{a} în ce m\u{a}sur\u{a} \c{s}i cu ce
cost testarea ghidat\u{a} de acoperire dep\u{a}\c{s}e\c{s}te generarea aleatoare. Propunem un cadru în Scala în
care o strategie este o compunere de tactici independente, ghidate de acoperire, peste o baz\u{a} onest\u{a}
identic\u{a} cu ScalaCheck. Studiem trei tactici: un \emph{pool} de constante extrase din surs\u{a}, o
\emph{muta\c{t}ie} la nivel de valoare \c{s}i un \emph{gradient} care coboar\u{a} o metric\u{a} de distan\c{t}\u{a} la
ramur\u{a}. Pe un benchmark de 35 de metode \c{s}i 30 de seminte, compunerea complet\u{a} atinge o acoperire
median\u{a} de $90.2\%$ fa\c{t}\u{a} de $55$–$62\%$ pentru aleator ($\hat{A}_{12}=1.0$), la un cost de doar
$\sim\!1.5\times$. Fiecare tactic\u{a} are o ni\c{s}\u{a} distinct\u{a}.

% ====================================================================
\chapter{Introduction}
\label{ch:intro}

As software touches more and more areas of our daily lives, ensuring its correctness has become an
important ongoing research subject and a real challenge. We start by establishing the broader context of
the software-testing research area, looking at different ways in which software validation is approached
today. We then narrow down to one specific problem that we address while emphasising both the objectives
and the main research question of the paper. In the end, we present the proposed solution through a
high-level description and provide a short overview of our contributions and results, as well as a
description of the structure of the rest of the paper.

\section{Context and Motivation}
\label{sec:context}

Since the beginning of engineering, its role in human life has progressively expanded, shaping nearly
every aspect of modern society. An important part of innovation is represented by software systems, which
have become deeply embedded in applications ranging from simple tools to critical domains. The likelihood
of major issues — unintended behaviour, bugs, errors, or vulnerabilities — grows as these systems become
more complex; such undiscovered issues can lead to costly consequences or significant risks~\cite{nist2002}.

Widely adopted solutions for validating software include testing and formal verification, used as
practical means of increasing confidence that a system behaves as intended. Both Myers et al.~\cite{myers}
and Claessen and Hughes~\cite{claessen2000} report that roughly half of the time spent in software
development is invested in testing. Figure~\ref{fig:confidence} arranges several validation techniques
along an axis of increasing confidence from left to right.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\fbox{\parbox{0.9\textwidth}{\centering\vspace{2mm}
\textsf{Manual testing} \quad$\longrightarrow$\quad \textsf{Property-based testing} \quad$\longrightarrow$\quad \textsf{Formal verification}\vspace{2mm}}}
\caption{Confidence in correctness, from sampled inputs to mathematical proof.}
\label{fig:confidence}
\end{figure}

At the leftmost end lies manual testing, a general term that includes unit, functional, integration, and
end-to-end testing. The programmer selects specific input values based on personal judgement, executes the
program, and checks the output against the expected result. While straightforward and widely used in
industry, the confidence it provides is limited by the developer's ability to anticipate meaningful
scenarios~\cite{garousi}. At the opposite end stands formal verification, where validation is done by
mathematical proof rather than by sampling: one can demonstrate that a property holds universally. This
offers the highest confidence, but the proof effort often exceeds the implementation effort, and formal
methods have not been widely adopted in industry ``except, arguably, in the development of critical
systems in certain domains''~\cite{woodcock}.

Between the two lies property-based testing (PBT), a family of methods that combines elements of both. As
in formal verification, the developer writes general properties the system should satisfy; but rather than
proving them, PBT attempts to falsify them by automatically generating a large number of inputs and
executing the program in search of counterexamples~\cite{claessen2000}. PBT variants differ in how inputs
are generated: the simplest draws them at random, while more sophisticated strategies use runtime metrics
to guide subsequent values. This thesis concerns one strategy from the latter category — \emph{coverage-guided}
property-based testing — which uses runtime coverage information to influence the next generated inputs. We
discuss the limitations of purely random generation, contrast them with coverage-guided generation,
develop a framework that puts the approach into practice, and compare the two in terms of code coverage on
a benchmark designed to highlight hard-to-reach branches.

\section{Problem}
\label{sec:problem}

To obtain meaningful coverage, property-based testing relies almost entirely on generators to provide the
input values fed to the system under test (SUT). A property can only be exercised by inputs that actually
reach the relevant branches, so the overall coverage of a function depends directly on the quality of the
generated inputs. Many PBT frameworks treat the SUT as a black box and generate inputs randomly and
independently of one another. This is simple, popular, and often effective for shallow behaviour, but it
struggles with deep program paths and with highly constrained or structured inputs. As Hamlet~\cite{hamlet}
observes, random testing does not target any particular part of the program; whether more or fewer paths
are executed depends entirely on chance.

The fundamental issue is that no information about the code under test is taken into account, which can
lead to poor results and significant wasted effort beyond a certain point. Even if intuition suggests that
feeding runtime information back into the generators should help, the actual benefits are not immediately
evident. Identifying the conditions under which this idea brings advantages, and measuring how substantial
or negligible the benefits are in different cases, are among the main subjects studied in this thesis.

\section{Research Questions}
\label{sec:rq}

This thesis explores how a coverage-guided property-based testing framework compares to one that uses a
purely random generation strategy. The two focus areas are how much coverage each strategy can ultimately
achieve and how efficiently it gets there, measured by the number of inputs required. The study is guided
by one main research question:

\begin{quote}\itshape
\textbf{Main Question.} To what extent and at what cost does coverage-guided property-based testing improve
over a purely random strategy, both in terms of the code coverage ultimately achieved and the number of
inputs required to reach it?
\end{quote}

To answer it, we explore the following auxiliary questions:
\begin{enumerate}[label=RQ\arabic*., leftmargin=2.2cm]
  \item What are the limitations of purely random input generation in PBT frameworks, and in which kinds of tested code are those limitations emphasised?
  \item How can runtime coverage information from the SUT be collected during execution, and how can it be fed back to the input generators?
  \item How can coverage information be used by different generation strategies to create inputs that are effective at exploring new branches?
  \item On what classes of programs does the coverage-guided approach yield the largest improvements over the random baseline, considering both coverage and the number of inputs required?
  \item What overhead does a coverage-guided framework introduce, and under what circumstances is the effort outweighed by the benefits?
\end{enumerate}

\section{Objectives}
\label{sec:objectives}

The objectives of the project follow directly from the research questions:
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
  \item Build a framework in which random generation is an \emph{honest} baseline — identical to a standard PBT tool — so any measured difference is attributable to guidance and not to an unfair re-implementation.
  \item Collect branch-coverage feedback from the SUT at runtime, after every input, and expose it to the generators (RQ2).
  \item Design several coverage-guided generation strategies that exploit this feedback in different ways, and a uniform abstraction under which they compose (RQ3).
  \item Construct a benchmark that isolates the kinds of branches each strategy is meant to address, and quantify — with appropriate statistics — when guidance helps and by how much (RQ1, RQ4).
  \item Measure the runtime overhead the guidance introduces, to judge when it is worthwhile (RQ5).
\end{enumerate}

\section{Proposed Solution}
\label{sec:proposed-intro}

We propose a Scala framework in which a \emph{strategy} is a composition of independent \emph{tactics}, each
of which reads live coverage feedback to bias the next input. The empty composition is plain random
generation and is implemented as literally stock ScalaCheck, so the baseline is beyond suspicion. Three
tactics are studied: a constant \emph{pool} that injects literals mined from the source code; a value-level
\emph{mutation} engine that perturbs inputs which previously grew coverage; and a \emph{gradient} that derives a
numeric branch-distance metric from the conditionals and hill-climbs it toward the nearest uncovered
branch. Because every tactic follows the same interface, the eight strategies we evaluate are exactly the
eight subsets of these three tactics, which turns the comparison into a clean ablation study.

\section{Obtained Results}
\label{sec:results-intro}

On a benchmark of 35 methods across six categories, evaluated over $K{=}30$ seeds, the full composition
reaches a median branch coverage of $90.2\%$ against $60.4\%$ for the random baseline (a $+29.8$ percentage-point
gain). Every guided strategy beats random on every seed (Vargha--Delaney $\hat{A}_{12}=1.0$, Mann--Whitney
$p\approx 2.3\!\times\!10^{-11}$). Each tactic owns a distinct niche — the pool cracks exact-literal gates,
the gradient cracks computed numeric targets, and mutation cracks incrementally-built structured inputs —
and only the composition covers a category whose arms each demand a different tactic. The guidance costs
about $1.5\times$ in throughput, because the dominant per-input cost (reading the coverage instrumentation) is
shared by all strategies. On externally sourced code we did not design, the tactics help exactly where their
mechanism matches the program, and several branches stay hard for everyone.

\section{Contributions}
\label{sec:contributions}

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
  \item A unifying \emph{tactic} abstraction that expresses literal pooling, mutation, and branch-distance search as interchangeable, coverage-guided components over one value-level generator type class, with the random baseline preserved exactly (Chapter~\ref{ch:solution}).
  \item A faithful re-use of the host PBT loop (ScalaCheck) together with a span-containment coverage reader built on \texttt{scoverage} and a static branch-tree extractor built on Scalameta, including a small condition language and an autonomous branch-distance gradient (Chapter~\ref{ch:impl}).
  \item A purpose-built benchmark organised as a progression of niches, and a rigorous $K{=}30$ evaluation reporting effect size ($\hat{A}_{12}$) and significance (Mann--Whitney $U$) per the Arcuri--Briand protocol~\cite{arcuri2014} (Chapter~\ref{ch:eval}).
  \item An empirical characterisation of \emph{when} each mechanism helps, including an external-validity study on code we did not author, and two structural findings — that the framework's lack of crossover prevents a single branch from requiring two tactics at once, and that mutation contributes only when concentrated — that we believe generalise beyond this implementation.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Structure}
\label{sec:structure}

The rest of the paper is structured as follows. Chapter~\ref{ch:background} introduces the technical
concepts and necessary background. Chapter~\ref{ch:related} positions the paper in the broader research
landscape. Chapter~\ref{ch:solution} introduces the high-level proposed solution and the design ideas
behind it, while Chapter~\ref{ch:impl} delves into the technologies used and the low-level implementation.
Chapter~\ref{ch:eval} introduces the evaluation benchmark and discusses the obtained results.
Chapter~\ref{ch:conclusions} concludes by presenting general findings, limitations, and future work.

% ====================================================================
\chapter{Background}
\label{ch:background}

This chapter presents the concepts required to understand the ideas behind coverage-guided property-based
testing. We introduce the vocabulary of software testing, narrow down to property-based testing and its
central concepts — properties and generators — then present code coverage as a measure of how thoroughly a
test exercises a program, and finally the two ideas that guidance borrows from neighbouring fields: search-based
testing with a branch-distance metric, and mutation-based, coverage-guided fuzzing.

\section{Software Testing}
\label{sec:testing}

When developing a software system, writing functional code is only one step of the process; testing comes
immediately after. Myers et al.~\cite{myers} describe testing as ``the process of executing a program with
the intent of finding errors''. Testing methods are classified by several criteria. By the amount of code
exercised, we distinguish unit testing (isolating a single behaviour), integration testing (combining
several components), and end-to-end testing (validating system specifications). Orthogonally, by the
knowledge of the implementation, we distinguish \emph{black-box} testing, which considers only the
specification, from \emph{white-box} (or structural) testing, which uses knowledge of the code's internal
structure. The distinction is central to this thesis: a purely random generator is black-box, whereas a
coverage-guided generator is grey-box — it does not read the source semantics but does observe which parts
of the code each input reaches.

Every test needs a \emph{test oracle}: a mechanism that decides whether an execution is correct. In manual
testing the oracle is the developer's hand-written expected output. As we shall see, property-based testing
replaces per-input oracles with general properties.

\section{Property-Based Testing}
\label{sec:pbt}

Property-based testing was popularised by QuickCheck~\cite{claessen2000}. Instead of writing individual
input/output pairs, the developer states a \emph{property} — a predicate that should hold for all inputs —
and a \emph{generator} produces many inputs at which the property is checked. If any input falsifies the
property, it is reported as a counterexample. A canonical property is that reversing a list twice yields
the original list, $\forall xs.\ \mathit{reverse}(\mathit{reverse}(xs)) = xs$, checked on hundreds of
randomly generated lists.

Two components matter for this work. First, the \emph{generator}: a description of how to sample values of a
type. ScalaCheck exposes generators through the \texttt{Gen[A]} type and a type class \texttt{Arbitrary[A]}
that supplies a default generator for a type. Generators are biased toward small and edge values (empty
collections, $0$, $\pm 1$, extreme integers) because such values frequently expose faults. Second,
\emph{shrinking}: when a counterexample is found, the framework searches for a smaller one that still fails,
to ease debugging. Shrinking is irrelevant to coverage measurement, and we deliberately disable it
(Section~\ref{sec:loop}) so that every generated input is counted exactly once.

A property may carry a \emph{precondition} of the form $P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)$. When $P$ is sparse — for
instance ``$xs$ is sorted'' — most random inputs fail $P$ and are discarded, so the body $Q$ is rarely
exercised. This precondition problem motivates much of the related work but is orthogonal to our setting,
in which we measure branch coverage of total functions rather than the discard rate of a sparse
precondition.

\section{Code Coverage}
\label{sec:coverage}

Code coverage quantifies how thoroughly a test suite exercises a program. The common criteria form a
hierarchy of increasing strictness:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em]
  \item \textbf{Statement coverage}: each statement executed at least once.
  \item \textbf{Branch (decision) coverage}: each edge of every decision taken at least once — e.g.\ both the \texttt{then} and the \texttt{else} of an \texttt{if}.
  \item \textbf{Path coverage}: every distinct path through the control-flow graph, generally infeasible because the number of paths is exponential.
\end{itemize}
This thesis uses \textbf{branch coverage}, the level at which the difference between strategies is most
visible: random generation typically reaches the ``easy'' side of each decision quickly but may never
reach the ``hard'' side. Coverage is collected by \emph{instrumentation}: the SUT is compiled with extra
bookkeeping code so that, at runtime, the set of executed program points can be read back. We use
\texttt{scoverage}, the standard statement-coverage instrumenter for Scala, and lift its statement-level
measurements to branch coverage by source-span containment (Section~\ref{sec:cov-impl}).

\section{Search-Based Testing and Branch Distance}
\label{sec:sbt}

Reaching a branch guarded by an exact numeric condition — say \texttt{x*x == 49} — is hopeless for uniform
random sampling. Search-based software testing reframes the task as optimisation: define an objective
function, the \emph{branch distance}, that measures how far an input is from satisfying a condition, and
minimise it. Korel~\cite{korel1990} introduced branch distances for the relational operators; for example,
the distance to satisfy $a = b$ is $|a-b|$, and the distance to satisfy $a < b$ is $0$ if it already holds
and $a-b+1$ otherwise. Tools such as EvoSuite~\cite{fraser2011} combine such distances with evolutionary
search to generate unit tests for the JVM. Our gradient tactic adapts this idea to a property-based setting,
deriving the distance automatically from the source and hill-climbing it (Section~\ref{sec:gradient}).

\section{Mutation-Based, Coverage-Guided Fuzzing}
\label{sec:cgf}

Coverage-guided fuzzing, exemplified by AFL~\cite{afl}, takes the opposite, undirected approach: it keeps a
corpus of inputs that expanded coverage and repeatedly \emph{mutates} them, retaining any mutant that
reaches something new. The bet is \emph{locality} — that inputs near an interesting input are themselves
likely to be interesting — so a retained seed becomes a springboard that the search ratchets forward. AFL
mutates at the level of raw bytes; type-aware variants instead mutate structured values. A related idea is
the AFL \emph{dictionary}: a list of useful constants (magic numbers, tokens) spliced into inputs. Our
mutation tactic adapts coverage-guided mutation to value-level Scala data, and our pool tactic adapts the
dictionary idea by mining constants from the program text.

% ====================================================================
\chapter{Related Work}
\label{ch:related}

Following the faculty's guidance, we separate the \emph{state of the art} — the current capabilities of
input-generation research — from \emph{related work}, the specific lines of activity that advance it and
against which we position our contribution.

\section{Property-Based Generators}

QuickCheck~\cite{claessen2000} established the model of properties plus type-directed random generators and
shrinking; ScalaCheck is its Scala descendant and the host of our framework. A recurring limitation is that
hand-written, highly tuned generators are needed to reach demanding inputs, which requires substantial
manual effort. FEAT~\cite{duregard2012} addresses enumeration of algebraic data types systematically rather
than randomly. These approaches remain black-box with respect to the SUT.

\section{Feedback-Directed and Search-Based Test Generation}

Randoop~\cite{pacheco2007} pioneered \emph{feedback-directed} random testing for object-oriented programs:
it builds method sequences incrementally, using the feedback of previous executions to avoid redundant or
illegal sequences. EvoSuite~\cite{fraser2011} generates whole unit-test suites for Java by evolutionary
search over branch-distance fitness, and is the closest ancestor of our gradient tactic, though it targets
test-suite generation rather than property falsification. Targeted property-based testing by L\"oscher and
Sagonas~\cite{loscher2017} adds a search component to PBT, guiding generation toward a user-supplied
utility value; our gradient differs in being \emph{autonomous} — it targets whatever branch is currently
uncovered, with no user-specified objective.

\section{Coverage-Guided Fuzzing and Property Testing}

AFL~\cite{afl} and libFuzzer brought coverage-guided mutation to the mainstream for byte-oriented programs.
The work closest to ours is coverage-guided property-based testing (CGPT), implemented as FuzzChick by
Lampropoulos et al.~\cite{lampropoulos2019}, which extends QuickChick (Coq's QuickCheck) with type-aware
mutation of coverage-expanding seeds and a power schedule. Our mutation tactic is directly inspired by it.
The differences are instructive and are revisited throughout the thesis: (i) FuzzChick targets sparse
\emph{preconditions} via two seed queues, whereas we measure branch coverage of total functions and need no
discard queue; (ii) FuzzChick is a single integrated loop, whereas we expose mutation as one tactic among
three independent, composable ones; and (iii) we add two mechanisms FuzzChick does not have — source-literal
pooling and a branch-distance gradient. Zest~\cite{padhye2019zest} and the JQF platform~\cite{padhye2019jqf}
combine coverage feedback with \emph{validity} feedback to fuzz programs with structured inputs; we identify
validity-guided generation as the natural next step for the branches our framework cannot reach
(Section~\ref{sec:realworld}).

\section{Constant Mining}

The idea of harvesting constants from the program and injecting them is used by AFL's dictionary~\cite{afl}
and, in the PBT setting, by DRAGEN2~\cite{mista2019}, which derives generators and mines literals from data
declarations. Our pool tactic mines \emph{every} literal in the method body and injects it through the
generator, type-matched, while remaining coverage-guided in that it stands down once all branches are
covered.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering\small
\caption{Positioning. Columns: literal mining, value-level mutation, branch-distance search, coverage feedback, honest random baseline, ablation of mechanisms.}
\label{tab:positioning}
\begin{tabular}{lcccccc}
\toprule
 & Pool & Mut. & Grad. & Cov. fb. & Honest base & Ablation \\
\midrule
QuickCheck/ScalaCheck~\cite{claessen2000} & -- & -- & -- & -- & n/a & -- \\
Randoop~\cite{pacheco2007}                & -- & seq. & -- & \checkmark & -- & -- \\
EvoSuite~\cite{fraser2011}                & -- & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & -- & -- \\
Targeted PBT~\cite{loscher2017}           & -- & \checkmark & \checkmark & -- & -- & -- \\
FuzzChick / CGPT~\cite{lampropoulos2019}  & -- & \checkmark & -- & \checkmark & \checkmark & -- \\
Zest / JQF~\cite{padhye2019zest}          & -- & byte & -- & \checkmark & -- & -- \\
DRAGEN2~\cite{mista2019}                  & \checkmark & -- & -- & -- & -- & -- \\
\textbf{This thesis}                      & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

% ====================================================================
\chapter{Proposed Solution}
\label{ch:solution}

This chapter describes the idea and the design, deferring technologies and code to Chapter~\ref{ch:impl}.
The guiding principle is that any difference we report must be attributable to \emph{guidance}, and to
nothing else.

\section{An Honest Baseline}
\label{sec:honest}

A frequent methodological pitfall when claiming that a clever method beats a simple one is to compare
against a weakened version of the simple one. We avoid this by making the random strategy \emph{the host
framework itself}. The generation loop is ScalaCheck's own loop; the random strategy adds nothing to it.
Every guided strategy runs the identical loop and merely contributes additional proposals for the next
input. Consequently, ``random'' is not our re-implementation of random testing — it is stock ScalaCheck —
and every reported gain is the marginal effect of guidance.

\section{Tactics: A Uniform Abstraction}
\label{sec:tactics}

We factor guidance into independent \emph{tactics}. A tactic observes the live coverage feedback and may
\emph{propose} how to draw the next input; it may also \emph{observe} each executed input to maintain
internal state. A \emph{strategy} is a set of tactics. The empty set is the honest baseline; each non-empty
set blends its tactics' proposals with a plain random draw. Because the abstraction is uniform, the eight
strategies we evaluate are exactly the eight subsets of three tactics, which is what makes the study an
ablation rather than a comparison of unrelated tools.

\section{Three Complementary Mechanisms}
\label{sec:three}

The three tactics were chosen because they attack \emph{disjoint} kinds of hard branches.

\paragraph{Pool — exact-value gates.} Some branches are guarded by equality against a literal that does not
appear in the input distribution: \texttt{tag == "admin"}, \texttt{httpMethod == "POST"}, a map key. No
amount of random sampling produces the exact string, and no numeric search applies. The pool mines such
literals from the source and injects them, so the branch becomes reachable
(Figure~\ref{fig:pool}).

\paragraph{Gradient — computed numeric targets.} Other branches are guarded by a numeric relation over
\emph{computed} values, such as \texttt{x*x == 49} or \texttt{a*a + b*b == c*c}. Here injecting the literal
is useless (the input is $x$, not $49$), but the condition admits a smooth distance, so the branch is
reachable by hill-climbing (Figure~\ref{fig:gradient}).

\paragraph{Mutation — incrementally-built structure.} Finally, some branches require a whole structured
input — a long sorted list, a deep tree — that random cannot assemble in one shot but that can be built up
one coverage-rewarded step at a time. Mutation keeps a corpus of coverage-expanding seeds and perturbs them,
ratcheting toward the structure (Figure~\ref{fig:mutation}).

None of the three subsumes another, so the union is expected to dominate each part — the central hypothesis
of the thesis (Figure~\ref{fig:combined}).

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.40\textwidth]{../images/pool.png}\hfill
\includegraphics[width=0.40\textwidth]{../images/mutation.png}
\caption{The pool tactic (left) and the mutation tactic (right) composed with random. In every diagram, \emph{random} is one choice the strategy can pick, the chosen value is strongly typed, the instrumented SUT is run, and coverage feeds back into the tactic's state.}
\label{fig:pool}
\label{fig:mutation}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.40\textwidth]{../images/gradient.png}\hfill
\includegraphics[width=0.46\textwidth]{../images/combined.png}
\caption{The gradient tactic (left): keep the input closest, by branch distance, to an uncovered branch and nudge it. All four tactics together (right): each proposes into the same pick, and coverage feeds each tactic's state.}
\label{fig:gradient}
\label{fig:combined}
\end{figure}

\section{Value-Level Generation}
\label{sec:value-level}

Unlike byte-oriented fuzzers, our tactics operate on \emph{typed values}. A single type class describes,
for each type, how to draw a fresh value, how to mutate one, and how to splice in pooled literals. This
keeps mutations meaningful (a mutated list is still a list) and lets the gradient and mutation share the
same stepping operators. The design is open: a new input type plugs in by providing one instance, and a new
tactic by implementing one interface — nothing else changes.

% ====================================================================
\chapter{Implementation}
\label{ch:impl}

The framework is written in Scala~2.13 and split into two sbt modules: an instrumented module containing
the benchmark methods, and a dependency-light engine that hosts the framework and the experiment harness.
This chapter describes the test loop, how coverage is collected and matched to source, how the static
branch tree and the gradient's condition language are extracted, and how each tactic is realised.

\section{The Generation Loop is ScalaCheck's}
\label{sec:loop}

The core entry point runs a single ScalaCheck property whose generator re-reads the live feedback on every
draw (Listing~\ref{lst:loop}). \texttt{Gen.delay} defers the generator so that each input sees the current
feedback; \texttt{forAllNoShrink} disables shrinking; the always-true body snapshots coverage as a side
effect, records it, and lets stateful tactics learn. When the set of tactics is empty, \texttt{mix} returns
the plain \texttt{arbitrary} generator unchanged — the honest baseline.

\begin{lstlisting}[language=Scala,caption={The test loop. The random strategy is the \texttt{arbitrary} branch of \texttt{mix}, untouched.},label=lst:loop]
val gen  = Gen.delay(mix(g.arbitrary, tactics.flatMap(_.propose(feedback))))
val prop = Prop.forAllNoShrink(gen) { (input: A) =>
  try property(input) catch { case _: Throwable => () }
  val nowCovered = covered(leaves, coverage.firedOffsets(sourceFile))
  feedback = feedback.record(input, nowCovered)
  tactics.foreach(_.observe(input, feedback))
  true
}
Test.check(params.withInitialSeed(seed).withMinSuccessfulTests(inputs).withWorkers(1), prop)
\end{lstlisting}

The \texttt{mix} function blends proposals with the random draw using \texttt{Gen.frequency}; the random
draw keeps a fixed minority weight so it remains an escape hatch and seeds the corpus, while the guided
tactics receive the bulk of the draws — following the FuzzChick maxim that inputs should rarely be produced
by the plain generator. Because \texttt{scoverage}'s recording back end is process-global, each
(strategy, seed) pair is executed in its own forked JVM; a Makefile sweeps the pairs.

\section{Collecting and Matching Coverage}
\label{sec:cov-impl}

We hold two independent views of each method and join them by source position.

\paragraph{Runtime view.} The benchmark module is compiled with \texttt{coverageEnabled}, so \texttt{scoverage}
instruments every statement and, at runtime, appends the identifier of each fired statement to a measurement
file. After each input we \emph{tail} that append-only file — reading only newly written bytes — and map the
fired identifiers back to their source offsets. This is $O(\text{new ids})$ per input and cheap to call in
the hot loop.

\paragraph{Static view.} The method's source is parsed with Scalameta into a \emph{branch tree}: a tree of
decision nodes and \emph{leaves}, where a leaf is one distinct path through the body and is the unit we
count for branch coverage. Each leaf stores its full source span.

\paragraph{The join.} A leaf is covered when some fired offset lies inside its span. Matching by span
containment, rather than by exact offsets or by \texttt{scoverage}'s method attribution, proved robust to
the discrepancies that exact-offset matching suffered around constructs such as \texttt{yield} and nested
definitions. Nested definitions are treated as opaque — their internals are not expanded — so only the call
site is counted.

\section{Extracting the Gradient's Condition Language}
\label{sec:gradient}

The gradient needs to turn a Scala condition such as \texttt{x*x == 49} into a numeric formula it can
evaluate. We define two small languages — numeric expressions (\texttt{Par(i)}, \texttt{Num}, \texttt{Unary},
\texttt{Binary}) and conditions (\texttt{Cmp}, \texttt{And}, \texttt{Or}, \texttt{Not}) — and a recursive
translator that walks the Scalameta tree and produces them, mapping each parameter to its \emph{position}
(Listing~\ref{lst:condof}). Crucially the translation is partial: if any sub-term falls outside the numeric
fragment — a string, a collection, a method call — the whole condition collapses to \texttt{None} and that
branch simply gets no gradient. This is what makes the gradient ``numeric only'' by construction.

\begin{lstlisting}[language=Scala,caption={Extracting a numeric condition (excerpt). \texttt{exprOf} is the analogous translator for number-valued sub-terms.},label=lst:condof]
private def condOf(t: Tree, env: Env): Option[Cond] = t match {
  case ApplyInfix(lhs, op, rhs) => op match {
    case "&&" => for (a <- condOf(lhs); b <- condOf(rhs)) yield And(a, b)
    case "||" => for (a <- condOf(lhs); b <- condOf(rhs)) yield Or(a, b)
    case "==" | "!=" | "<" | "<=" | ">" | ">=" =>
      for (a <- exprOf(lhs); b <- exprOf(rhs)) yield Cmp(op, a, b)
    case _ => None
  }
  case "!"(arg)      => condOf(arg).map(Not)
  case name: TermName => env.get(name) // a bare boolean parameter
  case _             => None
}
\end{lstlisting}

For each leaf, we then accumulate the conditions along the path that reaches it, keeping only leaves whose
\emph{entire} path is expressible. At runtime, an input is bound to its parameters by position (a tuple of
matching arity splits component-wise; non-numeric components become undefined), and the branch distance of a
condition is computed by the rules in Equation~\eqref{eq:dist}, where smaller means closer and $0$ means
satisfied:

\begin{equation}
\label{eq:dist}
d(a = b) = |a-b|,\quad
d(a < b) = \begin{cases}0 & a<b\\ a-b+1 & \text{otherwise}\end{cases},\quad
d(p \wedge q) = d(p)+d(q),\quad
d(p \vee q) = \min(d(p), d(q)).
\end{equation}

The distances are left un-normalised so that large gaps still slope toward the target. A path's fitness is
the sum of its conditions' distances. The \texttt{GradientTactic} keeps the input with the smallest positive
fitness toward the nearest uncovered leaf and, while that fitness is positive, proposes a mutation of it —
a stochastic hill-climb. For \texttt{x*x == 49}, the distances at $x=5,6,7$ are $24, 13, 0$, so the search
descends $5 \to 6 \to 7$ and covers the branch, even though the literal $49$ never helps directly.

\section{The Pool and Mutation Tactics}
\label{sec:pool-mut}

\paragraph{Pool.} At parse time we mine \emph{every} literal in the method body into a typed constant pool;
over-approximating is cheap, since a literal that fits no branch is merely a wasted draw, whereas a missed
literal silently neuters the tactic. The pool tactic injects these literals through the generator,
type-matched, on a fixed fraction of draws, and stands down once every leaf is covered — its coverage-guided
handle.

\paragraph{Mutation.} The mutation tactic perturbs the corpus of inputs that previously grew coverage. The
single most important detail is the \emph{power schedule}: it mutates the \emph{most recent} coverage-grower —
the input on the current coverage frontier — with high probability, falling back to an older seed only
occasionally. Because a mutant that breaks the frontier covers nothing new and is therefore not retained,
the search keeps springing off the best seed. Without this bias, uniform sampling over the corpus made
mutation spin on shallow seeds and fail to climb the structured benchmarks; with it, mutation reaches them.
The per-type mutators are value-level: integers take multi-scale $\pm 2^k$ steps plus edge values; strings
insert, delete, or substitute a character; lists mutate, drop, or prepend an element; trees grow a leaf into
a node, recurse, or prune; and tuples mutate exactly one component, holding the rest — the operator that lets
the search freeze a hard-won coordinate and work on another.

\section{The Benchmark and the Tooling}
\label{sec:bench-impl}

The benchmark is organised as a progression of categories, each isolating one tactic's niche
(Section~\ref{sec:bench-design}). The experiment harness runs every method for one (strategy, seed) pair
and writes a JSON report per cell; a Python script renders per-cell branch-tree figures and the
cross-strategy charts and statistics. A separate \texttt{Probe} runner explores individual methods at a chosen
input budget without writing the reports, summarising the result as a single median table across seeds — used
for the external-validity study.

% ====================================================================
\chapter{Evaluation}
\label{ch:eval}

\section{Benchmark Design}
\label{sec:bench-design}

A benchmark for this question must isolate \emph{kinds} of hard branches, so that an improvement can be
attributed to a mechanism rather than to luck. We therefore designed 35 methods across six categories as a
deliberate progression (Table~\ref{tab:categories}). \emph{Saturated} methods are solved by random alone and
calibrate the floor. \emph{Literals} are gated by exact strings, option payloads, and map keys — the pool's
territory. \emph{NumericSearch} gates on computed or relational numeric targets that injecting the literal
cannot satisfy — the gradient's territory. \emph{Sequences} require ordered lists or tall trees built up
incrementally — mutation's territory. \emph{Mixed} methods have several arms, each a different tactic's job, so
only the composition covers them all. Finally, \emph{RealWorld} contains canonical implementations lifted from
well-known specifications, whose control flow we did \emph{not} design — the external-validity check.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering\small
\caption{Benchmark categories and their intended owner.}
\label{tab:categories}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
Category & Hard-branch kind & Intended owner \\
\midrule
Saturated     & shallow / edge values            & random (floor) \\
Literals      & exact string / option / map key  & pool \\
NumericSearch & computed / relational numeric    & gradient \\
Sequences     & ordered list / deep tree         & mutation \\
Mixed         & several arms, one per tactic      & composition \\
RealWorld     & external code (parsers, dates)    & whoever matches \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section{Methodology}
\label{sec:methodology}

Each method is run for $10{,}000$ inputs per (strategy, seed), each pair in its own forked JVM, for
$K{=}30$ seeds — the conventional minimum for assessing randomised algorithms. Because both the baseline and
the strategies are randomised, ``the median is higher'' is not a claim on its own; following Arcuri and
Briand~\cite{arcuri2014} we report, for every strategy against random, the Vargha--Delaney $\hat{A}_{12}$
effect size — the probability that a run of the strategy beats a run of random — and the two-sided
Mann--Whitney $U$ $p$-value, both over the $30$ per-seed coverage percentages. We additionally report a
\emph{time-to-coverage} curve (coverage as a function of the input budget), a \emph{blind-spot fill} metric
(of the leaves random never reached, how many a strategy did), and \emph{throughput} (inputs per second), to
answer the cost side of the main question.

\section{Overall Results}
\label{sec:overall}

Table~\ref{tab:suite} reports suite-wide median coverage. Every guided strategy strictly dominates random:
$\hat{A}_{12}=1.000$ (the strategy wins on \emph{every} one of the $30$ seeds) with $p\approx 2.3$–$2.6\times10^{-11}$.
The full composition reaches $90.2\%$ against the baseline's $60.4\%$, a $+29.8$ percentage-point improvement,
which answers the coverage half of the main question.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering\small
\caption{Suite-wide median coverage over $K{=}30$ seeds, and effect size / significance versus random.}
\label{tab:suite}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
Strategy & Median cov. & $\hat{A}_{12}$ & Mann--Whitney $p$ \\
\midrule
random (baseline)                     & 60.4\% & --    & -- \\
coverage-guided (gradient)            & 68.0\% & 1.000 & $2.6\times10^{-11}$ \\
mutation-guided                       & 69.5\% & 1.000 & $2.6\times10^{-11}$ \\
random-pool                           & 74.4\% & 1.000 & $2.3\times10^{-11}$ \\
coverage-guided-pool                  & 81.1\% & 1.000 & $2.4\times10^{-11}$ \\
mutation-guided-pool                  & 84.8\% & 1.000 & $2.4\times10^{-11}$ \\
\textbf{all four (composition)}       & \textbf{90.2\%} & \textbf{1.000} & $\mathbf{2.4\times10^{-11}}$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section{Per-Niche Results (RQ1, RQ4)}
\label{sec:niches}

Table~\ref{tab:niches} confirms that each category is owned by its intended tactic. \emph{Literals} jumps
from $33\%$ to $100\%$ the moment the pool is enabled, and not otherwise. \emph{NumericSearch} reaches $100\%$
only with the gradient. \emph{Sequences} reaches $100\%$ only with mutation. \emph{Mixed} reaches $100\%$ only
for the composition, since its arms each need a different tactic. This directly answers RQ4: the largest
improvements occur exactly where the program's hard branches match a tactic's mechanism, and the composition
is never worse than its best component.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering\small
\caption{Per-category median coverage ($K{=}30$). Each category is owned by a distinct tactic; only the composition wins Mixed.}
\label{tab:niches}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc c}
\toprule
Category & rand & +pool & +mut & +grad & all \\
\midrule
Saturated     & 100 & 100 & 100 & 100 & 100 \\
Literals      & 33  & \textbf{100} & 33  & 33  & 100 \\
NumericSearch & 67  & 67  & 75  & \textbf{100} & 100 \\
Sequences     & 78  & 78  & \textbf{100} & 78  & 100 \\
Mixed         & 50  & 75  & 75  & 75  & \textbf{100} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section{Efficiency and Cost (RQ5)}
\label{sec:cost}

The time-to-coverage curve shows that guidance is not only higher but \emph{faster}: at a budget of $1{,}000$
inputs the composition already reaches $\sim\!82\%$, a level random does not reach even at $10{,}000$. The
overhead is modest: median throughput falls from $\sim\!145{,}000$ inputs/s for random to $\sim\!98{,}000$
for the full composition — a factor of about $1.5\times$. The reason is structural: the dominant per-input
cost, reading the coverage instrumentation, is shared by every strategy, so the tactics add only their own
small overhead on top. This answers RQ5: for a $+29.8$ percentage-point coverage gain (and far fewer inputs
to reach any given level), a $1.5\times$ slowdown is comfortably worthwhile.

\section{External Validity (RQ4)}
\label{sec:realworld}

The categories above are, by construction, favourable to the tactics. The \emph{RealWorld} category tests the
opposite: seven canonical functions (an \texttt{atoi} parser, a valid-number scanner, roman-numeral
conversion, version comparison, IPv4 validation, Gregorian date validation, and a Luhn checksum) whose
control flow we did not shape. The findings are honest and informative (Table~\ref{tab:realworld}): the pool
wins \texttt{isValidDate}, whose branches are gated by the month and year literals it mines; mutation wins
\texttt{isValidNumber}, whose scanner states are reached by editing partially-valid strings; and three
functions — \texttt{atoi}, \texttt{isValidIPv4}, \texttt{luhn} — remain hard for \emph{every} strategy, even at
one million inputs, because their deep branches require a structurally valid input (an overflowing integer,
a dotted quad, a valid checksum) that none of our mechanisms synthesises. This is precisely the gap that
validity-guided generation addresses, and it motivates our main item of future work.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering\small
\caption{External-validity probe (median \%, one million inputs). The tactics help where their mechanism matches; some branches stay hard for all.}
\label{tab:realworld}
\begin{tabular}{lccccl}
\toprule
Method & rand & pool & mut & all & note \\
\midrule
isValidDate    & 43 & \textbf{86} & 71 & 86 & pool: mines month/year literals \\
isValidNumber  & 60 & 60 & \textbf{80} & 60 & mutation: builds number strings \\
romanToInt     & 100 & 100 & 100 & 100 & saturates at high budget \\
compareVersion & 100 & 100 & 100 & 100 & saturates at high budget \\
atoi           & 50 & 50 & 50 & 50 & hard for all \\
isValidIPv4    & 20 & 20 & 20 & 20 & hard for all \\
luhn           & 67 & 67 & 67 & 67 & hard for all \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section{Two Structural Findings}
\label{sec:findings}

Two results emerged that we believe transcend this implementation. First, the framework has \emph{no
crossover}: a single branch cannot require two tactics at once, because there is no operator that merges, say,
a pool-found string with a gradient-found number into one input. Tactics therefore compose by covering
\emph{different} branches, not by combining on one — which is why the Mixed category is built from separate
single-tactic arms. Second, \emph{mutation contributes only when concentrated}: it climbs a hard structural gate
only when it receives the bulk of the draws (its own strategy), so in a diluted composition it cannot, and
its niche is the dedicated Sequences category. Both findings constrain how coverage-guided tactics can be
combined and are, to our knowledge, not articulated in the prior PBT literature.

\section{Threats to Validity}
\label{sec:threats}

\textbf{Construct.} We measure branch coverage, a proxy for fault detection rather than fault detection
itself; the RealWorld study mitigates but does not eliminate this. \textbf{Internal.} Both baseline and
strategies are randomised; we control for this with $K{=}30$ seeds and the Arcuri--Briand statistics, and the
honest baseline removes the most common confound. \textbf{External.} Five of six categories are
author-designed and therefore favourable; the RealWorld category exists specifically to test whether the
mechanisms transfer to code we did not shape, and the results there are reported as they are, including the
cases where no tactic helps. The study is confined to one language (Scala) and small methods; generalisation
to large systems and other languages is future work.

% ====================================================================
\chapter{Conclusions and Future Work}
\label{ch:conclusions}

\section{Conclusions}

This thesis set out to determine \emph{to what extent and at what cost} coverage-guided property-based testing
improves over purely random generation. We answer the main question affirmatively and quantitatively: on a
benchmark of 35 methods over $30$ seeds, composing three coverage-guided tactics over an honest ScalaCheck
baseline raised median branch coverage from $60.4\%$ to $90.2\%$, with a maximal effect size
($\hat{A}_{12}=1.0$) and overwhelming significance, at a throughput cost of only about $1.5\times$. We also
answer each auxiliary question. \textbf{RQ1}: random generation fails on exact-literal, computed-numeric, and
structured-input branches, and these are exactly where guidance helps most. \textbf{RQ2}: coverage can be
collected cheaply by tailing \texttt{scoverage}'s measurement file after every input and matched to the
source by span containment. \textbf{RQ3}: the same feedback supports three different mechanisms — literal
injection, value-level mutation with a power schedule, and branch-distance hill-climbing — unified by one
tactic interface. \textbf{RQ4}: each mechanism owns a distinct niche, the composition wins where several
niches meet, and on external code the tactics help precisely where the program's structure matches them.
\textbf{RQ5}: the overhead is small because the cost is dominated by the shared instrumentation, so guidance
is worthwhile whenever the program contains branches random struggles to reach.

\section{Future Work}

The clearest opportunity is \emph{validity-guided generation} in the style of Zest~\cite{padhye2019zest}: the
RealWorld functions our framework cannot crack are exactly those that demand a structurally valid input, and
a validity signal would let mutation ratchet toward it. A second direction is to add a \emph{crossover}
operator so that a single branch requiring two tactics — a pooled string and a sorted list — becomes
reachable, lifting the no-crossover limitation. A third is a richer \emph{power schedule} with per-seed energy,
which may let mutation contribute inside compositions rather than only in isolation. Finally, the construct
gap should be closed by evaluating fault detection directly (for instance via injected faults or mutation
testing) and by extending the study to larger, real-world systems and to other languages.
